name: Columbia
h1: Personal Injury Lawyer in Columbia, SC
meta_title: Columbia SC Personal Injury Lawyer | Roden Law | Free Consult
meta_description: Injured in Columbia, SC? Roden Law handles car, truck & pedestrian accident cases near I-20, I-26, Assembly St & Two Notch Rd. Free consultation. No fees unless we win.
court: Richland County Circuit Court (5th Judicial Circuit)
roads: The I-20 and I-26 interchange — known locally as 'Malfunction Junction' — sits at the center of the state's interstate system and recorded 91 collisions including 2 fatal crashes in a recent reporting period. The SCDOT's Carolina Crossroads project is implementing phased improvements, but the convergence of two busy interstates continues to create a hazardous environment. Assembly Street and Elmwood Avenue recorded 50 collisions with 10 resulting in injuries, making it one of the most dangerous urban intersections in the city. Two Notch Road is one of the longest and most heavily traveled corridors in the Columbia area, with the Sparkleberry Lane intersection identified as a persistent crash hotspot. Broad River Road near I-26 sees heavy rush-hour congestion and frequent rear-end collisions.
hospitals:
Prisma Health Richland Hospital (Level I Trauma Center, 641 beds) — 5 Richland Medical Park Dr, Columbia, SC 29203 — (803) 434-7000
Prisma Health Baptist Hospital — 1600 Marion St, Columbia, SC 29201 — (803) 296-5010
Prisma Health Children's Hospital–Midlands (Level II Pediatric Trauma) — 7 Richland Medical Park Dr, Columbia, SC 29203 — (803) 434-7000
Providence Health Downtown — 2435 Forest Dr, Columbia, SC 29204 — (803) 256-5300
landmarks: University of South Carolina, South Carolina State House, Riverbanks Zoo & Garden, Soda City Market, Five Points, The Vista, Congaree National Park, Colonial Life Arena, Fort Jackson, Williams-Brice Stadium
service_area: Serving downtown Columbia, Shandon, Five Points, The Vista, Rosewood, Eau Claire, Cottontown, Elmwood Park, Arsenal Hill, Melrose Heights, and surrounding Midlands communities.
content_hook:
Columbia is the capital of South Carolina and the second-largest city in the state, with approximately 137,000 residents and an 870,000-person metro area. The city sits at the confluence of three rivers and the intersection of three major interstates — I-20, I-26, and I-77 — creating some of the highest traffic volumes in the state. Richland County recorded 12,450 motor vehicle collisions in 2023, ranking it among the most crash-heavy counties in South Carolina. The SCDPS has identified 13 high-crash corridors in the Columbia area, including Broad River Road, Two Notch Road, and Farrow Road.

Roden Law's Columbia office at 1545 Sumter Street is located in the heart of downtown Columbia, minutes from major accident corridors and the Richland County Courthouse. Our personal injury attorneys handle cases involving crashes at Malfunction Junction, on Assembly Street, along Two Notch Road, and throughout the Midlands. If you've been injured in a Columbia accident, call (803) 219-2816 for a free consultation — we work on a contingency fee basis, meaning you pay nothing unless we recover compensation for you.

faqs.question: What are the most dangerous intersections in Columbia, SC?
faqs.answer: According to SCDPS data, the most dangerous intersections include the I-20/I-26 interchange ('Malfunction Junction'), I-26 at US-176 (195 collisions, 60 injuries), Assembly Street at Elmwood Avenue (50 collisions), and Taylor Street at Huger Street (49 collisions). The Two Notch Road corridor and Broad River Road near I-26 are also identified as high-crash corridors. If you've been injured at any of these locations, contact Roden Law at (803) 219-2816.

faqs.question: How long do I have to file a personal injury claim in Columbia?
faqs.answer: South Carolina has a 3-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims (S.C. Code § 15-3-530). You must file your lawsuit within 3 years of the accident date. However, evidence deteriorates and witnesses become harder to locate over time, so contacting an attorney as early as possible strengthens your case.

faqs.question: Which hospital should I go to after a car accident in Columbia?
faqs.answer: For severe or life-threatening injuries, Prisma Health Richland Hospital on Richland Medical Park Drive is the Midlands' only Level I Trauma Center and treats over 3,300 serious injuries annually. Providence Health on Forest Drive and Prisma Health Baptist Hospital downtown also have 24/7 emergency departments. For Lexington County accidents, Lexington Medical Center in West Columbia is another excellent option.

faqs.question: Where is Roden Law's Columbia office located?
faqs.answer: Our Columbia office is at 1545 Sumter Street, Suite B, Columbia, SC 29201. We are centrally located in downtown Columbia, just blocks from the Richland County Courthouse. We serve injury victims throughout Richland County, Lexington County, and the surrounding Midlands region. Call (803) 219-2816 for a free case review.

Lexington is one of the fastest-growing towns in South Carolina, with approximately 24,000 residents — a 32% increase from the 2010 census. The town's proximity to Lake Murray and its highly rated school system continue to attract families and retirees, but this growth has strained local roadways. US-1, Sunset Boulevard, and the SC-6 corridor all carry traffic volumes that exceed their original design capacity, leading to frequent accidents during rush hours and weekends.

Roden Law's Columbia office serves Lexington residents and handles personal injury cases filed in Lexington County Circuit Court. faqs.answer: If your accident occurred within Lexington County, your personal injury lawsuit will typically be filed in Lexington County Circuit Court (11th Judicial Circuit), located in the Town of Lexington. Roden Law's attorneys practice in both Richland and Lexington County courts and handle cases throughout the Columbia metro.

faqs.answer: Lexington Medical Center on Sunset Boulevard in West Columbia is the nearest major hospital and is ranked the #1 hospital in the Columbia metro by U.S. News & World Report. Its emergency department treats nearly 85,000 patients annually. The hospital is a short drive from most Lexington neighborhoods.

faqs.question: What is the statute of limitations for a car accident in Lexington County?
faqs.answer: South Carolina has a 3-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims (S.C. Code § 15-3-530). This applies throughout the state, including Lexington County. Missing this deadline means permanently losing your right to pursue compensation.

faqs.question: What if I was partially at fault for my Oak Grove accident?
faqs.answer: South Carolina uses a modified comparative fault rule — you can recover damages as long as you are less than 51% at fault. Your compensation is reduced by your percentage of fault. For example, if you're 30% at fault on a $100,000 claim, you recover $70,000. Our attorneys work to minimize any fault assigned to you.

faqs.question: How quickly should I contact an attorney after an accident?
faqs.answer: As soon as possible. While you have 3 years to file, critical evidence such as surveillance footage, vehicle black box data, and witness memories can deteriorate within days or weeks. Early attorney involvement also prevents costly mistakes with insurance adjusters. Call (803) 219-2816.

Despite its small size, the surrounding area's rural highways carry traffic at speeds that create serious accident risks. In May 2024, a fatal crash at the intersection of US-178 and D.E. Roden Law serves Pelion and rural Lexington County residents from our Columbia office. Call (803) 219-2816 for a free consultation.

faqs.question: Can Roden Law handle my case even though Pelion is far from Columbia?

faqs.question: What are common causes of rural road accidents near Pelion?
faqs.answer: Common causes include speed-related crashes on two-lane roads, failure to yield at rural intersections, limited sight lines around curves and hills, agricultural equipment conflicts, and impaired driving. Our attorneys understand the unique challenges of rural accident cases.
